title: 'Endgame' Reigns in Theaters, While Netflix Rules at Home—What's the Future? Plus ‘Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil, and Vile’ Director Joe Berlinger | Interview
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins on why 'Avengers: Endgame' has emptied the multiplex of everything else and pushed the smaller movies to Netflix, then Fennessey talks to documentarian Joe Berlinger about turning the Ted Bundy tapes into a Zac Efron feature.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Past $2.2 billion worldwide and second all-time in 11 days; its $145M third weekend would be a great opening for anything else, and the hosts argue it's swallowed the whole theatrical calendar.

films.title: Long Shot
films.note: The Lionsgate rom-com they'd both championed (and gave a Charlize Theron episode to) made about $10M — the exact 'wait and rent it at home' casualty they keep circling back to.

films.title: UglyDolls
films.note: STX's bid for a franchise-and-merchandising machine opened to $8.5M; Fennessey jokes about a 4K home viewing on his Sonos Beam.

films.title: The Intruder
films.note: Screen Gems thriller, the weekend's other studio release, at $11M.

films.title: Apollo 11
films.note: The rare specialty film to do real business this year ($8M) — because the IMAX release is 'halfway to an Epcot ride,' something you leave the house for.

films.title: Knock Down the House
films.note: The AOC-and-three-challengers doc that dropped on Netflix; Dobbins says it gave her the theater experience she wants but will never get the RBG-style rollout because it's streaming.

films.title: Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile
films.note: The week's Netflix launch and the interview subject — a Ted Bundy story told through his girlfriend, played by Lily Collins, with Zac Efron as Bundy. Debated as a test of whether opening weekend still means anything for a movie that never leaves the service.

films.title: Unplanned
films.note: Pure Flix's anti-abortion drama, $18M on a $6M budget — cited as the micro-targeting 'super-serve' strategy, same as Knock Down the House from the other side.

segments.heading: Joe Berlinger on 'Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile'

films.title: Extremely Wicked, Shockingly Evil and Vile
films.note: A blacklist script kicking around six or seven years (Jodie Foster once attached). Berlinger's note was to stop hiding that it's Bundy and instead build the relationship so the third-act reveal lands as betrayal — and to go darker than the script's 'Catch Me If You Can' tone. Read it to greenlit in about six weeks.

films.title: Blair Witch 2: Book of Shadows
films.note: The feature he disavowed after the studio recut it. He calls the original director's cut a prescient satire on blurring fiction and reality, gutted at the twelfth hour to add on-screen killings and needle drops for a soundtrack, tied to Artisan's 2000 IPO.

films.title: Brother's Keeper
films.note: The doc he and Bruce Sinofsky made to feel like a narrative film — dramatic structure, an opening title sequence, an original fiddle score — choices old-guard documentarians attacked and young filmmakers now take for granted. Nobody wanted to buy it at Sundance; they self-distributed.

films.title: Paradise Lost: The Child Murders at Robin Hood Hills
films.note: His example of the 'emotional truth' vs. 'literal truth' argument — you film six weeks of trial and use one hour, and you trust the filmmaker for that.

films.title: Metallica: Some Kind of Monster
films.note: Came out of the Blair Witch 2 funk; the band's creative and existential crisis mirrored his own. Paramount paid $3M just for the DVD rights at Sundance 2004.

films.title: Crude
films.note: The Amazon-pollution doc he resisted making until a trip down there — canned tuna in a poisoned river, a clean glass of water back home — made him feel he'd be a schmuck not to. An early Netflix (Red Envelope) equity project.

films.title: Tony Robbins: I Am Not Your Guru
films.note: His first full collaboration with Netflix; watching the hashtag trend worldwide from a hotel balcony sold him on their reach. More people mention it to him than all his other films combined.

films.title: Intent to Destroy
films.note: The Armenian-genocide doc he thinks is one of his best, met with a shrug even inside the industry — his example that the buzz never predicts the work.

films.title: The Conformist
films.note: His last-great-thing pick. A rewatch of the Bertolucci that let him lose himself despite the subtitles — the antidote to 'gotcha' film criticism he says lacks empathy.
